3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Chu, J., Ganson, K. T., Murray, S. B., Iyer, P., Gabriel, K. P., Garber, A. K., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2023). Contemporary screen time modalities and disruptive behavior disorders in children: A prospective cohort study. Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 64(1), 125-135.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry
3A_Bibliographic.notes: This is the same paper as candidate_id 58. The PDF at 10_1111_jcpp_13673.pdf is the same study by Nagata et al. on screen time and conduct disorder/ODD. Coding duplicated from paper 58.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed prospective data from the ABCD Study, a longitudinal study of brain development and adolescent health in 11,875 children recruited from 21 sites around the United States (U.S.)... Data analyzed are from the ABCD 3.0 release for the baseline (2016-2018, 9-10-years-old) and one-year follow-up (2017-2019, 10-11-years-old) assessments.

3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (weighted average hours/day) and categorical (<2 hours, 2-4 hours, >4 hours/day)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was determined based on child self-report using the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Participants answered questions about typical hours per day spent on six different screen modalities... separately for weekdays and weekend days... The weighted average was calculated as:[(weekday average x 5) + (weekend average x 2)]/7

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), parent-reported
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Conduct disorder: 1.9%; Oppositional defiant disorder: 6.3% at one-year follow-up
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The ABCD Study utilized the Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), a computerized tool for categorizing child and adolescent mental health concerns based on the DSM-5, for the assessment of conduct and oppositional defiant disorders.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with robust standard errors (prevalence ratios)
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, highest parent education, parent marital status, baseline conduct disorder symptoms, baseline oppositional defiant disorder symptoms, baseline major depressive disorder, family history of psychopathology
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression analyses using robust standard errors were conducted to calculate prevalence ratios estimating associations between baseline screen time (exposure variable) and conduct or oppositional defiant disorder at one-year follow-up (outcome variables), adjusting for confounders.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: total screen time (PR 1.07, 95% CI 1.03-1.11) was prospectively associated with higher prevalence of conduct disorder

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of social media
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each hour of social media (PR 1.62, 95% CI 1.39-1.87)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: total screen time (PR 1.05, 95% CI 1.03-1.08) was prospectively associated with higher prevalence of oppositional defiant disorder

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The effect sizes for total screen time were relatively small... Despite smaller effect sizes, screen use is an important exposure because it can be modifiable through intervention

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Health care providers should consider assessing for behavioral problems among children with high screen use and advise about potential risks associated with excessive screen time.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: Effect sizes are reported for each hour of screen exposure per day; therefore, total effects are greater with more hours per day of screen exposure.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Despite smaller effect sizes, screen use is an important exposure because it can be modifiable through intervention, whereas the ability to intervene on genetics and baseline symptoms may be more limited.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Each hour of total screen time per day was associated with a 7% higher prevalence of conduct disorder and a 5% higher prevalence of oppositional defiant disorder.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges small effect sizes but uses modifiable-factor and per-unit accumulation arguments to justify clinical recommendations. Same paper as candidate 58.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: This is the same paper as candidate_id 58 (same DOI: 10.1111/jcpp.13673)
